Name: spectral_routing_inference
Title: Spectral Stability-Aware Routing: Training-Free Layer Selection via Bounded Jacobian Perturbation for Latency-Constrained Inference
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that layer-wise inference routing decisions can be made deterministically at test-time by measuring local Jacobian spectral norms against a provably stable perturbation budget, without requiring training or parallel sampling. This enables strict latency guarantees while preserving end-to-end accuracy through PAC-Bayes-bounded routing error. The key insight is that spectral stability of layer transitions—not activation magnitude or gradient norm—is the minimal sufficient statistic for predicting safe layer skipping, and this can be computed via single-pass amortized Lanczos iteration within wall-clock budgets.
Related Work: Existing inference acceleration methods fall into three categories: (1) Early exit methods (BranchyNet, DEEBERT) require training auxiliary classifiers and offer no formal stability guarantees; (2) Layer pruning approaches (CoFi, Layer Drop) rely on heuristics or training-time decisions, lacking input-adaptive test-time routing; (3) Adaptive computation work (MoE routing, ViT token pruning) uses learned routing policies requiring gradient backprop or parallel sampling, violating latency constraints. Recent work on uncertainty quantification in inference (e.g., temperature scaling, ensemble methods) does not directly address layer selection or provide spectral stability bounds. Our proposal uniquely combines: (a) training-free routing via spectral analysis, (b) formal PAC-Bayes bounds on routing error, (c) strict amortized latency accounting with Lanczos overhead integrated into wall-clock budgets, and (d) single-pass sequential routing eliminating parallel sampling. This directly targets ML inference theory rather than being a heuristic engineering contribution.
Abstract: We propose a training-free, latency-aware framework for test-time layer routing that selects which layers to execute based on spectral stability analysis of layer-wise Jacobians. The core contribution is a deterministic routing algorithm that computes per-layer spectral norms via single-pass Lanczos iteration, comparing them against a PAC-Bayes-derived perturbation budget to decide layer skipping. Unlike learned routing policies, our approach requires no training overhead and guarantees strict latency bounds by amortizing spectral computation into wall-clock budgets. We establish formal stability guarantees: for any layer, if its Jacobian spectral norm remains below a threshold derived from input-dependent activation statistics and a user-specified accuracy loss tolerance, skipping that layer preserves end-to-end accuracy with high probability. We validate on ResNets, Vision Transformers, and LLM inference tasks, demonstrating Pareto improvements over uniform quantization and learned early-exit baselines. Crucially, we quantify Lanczos overhead as a fraction of layer compute, enforce single-pass execution to eliminate memory pressure, and provide deterministic perturbation-cost accounting. Our method isolates one high-impact contribution—spectral routing without training—while maintaining modular validation pathways for ablation across spectral estimators, budget calibration strategies, and model architectures.

Experiments.name: Baseline Spectral Stability Analysis
Experiments.description: For a given model (ResNet-50, ViT-B/16, LLaMA-7B), compute per-layer Jacobian spectral norms offline using power iteration (10 iterations per layer) on 500 random inputs. Establish empirical distribution of spectral norms across layers. Measure: (1) spectral norm variance across inputs (quantify activation-dependent perturbation), (2) correlation between layer spectral norm and downstream accuracy loss when layer is skipped, (3) wall-clock time for power iteration per layer as fraction of layer FLOPs. This establishes the mechanistic link between spectral stability and safe layer skipping.

Experiments.name: PAC-Bayes Routing Threshold Derivation
Experiments.description: Given a target accuracy loss tolerance ε and confidence δ, derive routing threshold τ(ε,δ) using PAC-Bayes bound: with probability 1-δ, if layer spectral norm < τ, skipping incurs accuracy loss < ε. Formally: τ = KL(ε||ε₀) / (2m) where KL is Kullback-Leibler divergence, ε₀ is empirical loss on calibration set, m is calibration set size. Implement threshold calibration on 1000 validation samples. Measure: (1) empirical coverage (fraction of test samples where predicted bound holds), (2) threshold tightness (ratio of predicted to actual loss), (3) sensitivity to δ (vary δ from 0.01 to 0.5), (4) computational cost of threshold computation (should be < 1% of inference).

Experiments.name: Single-Pass Lanczos Overhead Accounting
Experiments.description: Implement inline, amortized Lanczos iteration for spectral norm estimation. For each layer, perform k ∈ {1,3,5} Lanczos iterations (early stopping) during forward pass. Measure: (1) wall-clock latency per layer with k iterations vs. baseline (no routing), (2) accuracy of Lanczos estimate vs. full power iteration, (3) memory footprint (Lanczos requires storing k+1 vectors; quantify peak memory vs. layer activation size), (4) total amortized overhead as % of inference latency. Target: overhead < 5% for k=3. Vary batch size (1, 8, 32) to isolate memory-bandwidth effects.

Experiments.name: Input-Adaptive Routing Validation
Experiments.description: For each test input, compute its layer-wise spectral perturbation budget: for layer i, compute local Jacobian norm ||J_i||₂ and estimate input-dependent activation range [a_min, a_max]. Routing decision: skip layer i if ||J_i||₂ · (a_max - a_min) < τ. Compare three routing strategies: (1) static routing (fixed set of layers skipped for all inputs), (2) uncertainty-calibrated routing (use temperature-scaled confidence scores as proxy for input difficulty), (3) spectral routing (proposed). Metrics: (1) accuracy-latency Pareto curve for each strategy, (2) per-layer skip rate distribution (identify which layers are consistently safe to skip), (3) robustness to domain shift (test on out-of-distribution samples; measure if spectral routing degrades gracefully).

Experiments.name: Structured Perturbation Robustness
Experiments.description: Evaluate routing stability under controlled input perturbations. For each test input x, generate adversarial perturbations: (1) ℓ∞ ball of radius ε ∈ {0.01, 0.05, 0.1}, (2) Gaussian noise with σ ∈ {0.01, 0.05}, (3) natural augmentations (rotation, brightness). For each perturbed input, recompute routing decisions and measure: (1) routing consistency (fraction of layers with same skip/execute decision), (2) accuracy degradation under perturbation, (3) stability radius (largest ε where routing remains >90% consistent). Compare to baseline methods. This validates that spectral routing is robust to input-dependent variations.

Experiments.name: End-to-End Accuracy-Latency Trade-off
Experiments.description: On ImageNet (ResNet-50, ViT-B/16) and GLUE tasks (LLaMA-7B), sweep accuracy loss tolerance ε from 0.1% to 5%. For each ε, compute routing threshold τ(ε,δ=0.1), apply spectral routing, measure: (1) actual end-to-end accuracy, (2) wall-clock inference latency (measured on single GPU, batch size 1), (3) FLOPs saved, (4) memory peak usage. Plot Pareto frontier (accuracy vs. latency) and compare against: (1) uniform layer skipping (fixed number of layers), (2) learned early-exit (DEEBERT-style), (3) magnitude-based pruning (skip layers with small activation norms). Quantify: speedup (e.g., 1.3x), accuracy drop (e.g., <0.5%), latency variance across 100 runs (standard deviation).

Experiments.name: Ablation: Spectral Estimator Choice
Experiments.description: Validate that spectral norm is the minimal sufficient statistic. Compare routing based on: (1) layer Jacobian spectral norm (proposed), (2) activation magnitude (mean/max of layer output), (3) gradient norm (backprop-based; violates latency constraint but included for oracle comparison), (4) Fisher information (requires Hessian; computationally expensive). For each proxy, measure: (1) correlation with actual layer importance (measured by accuracy drop when layer is skipped), (2) computational cost, (3) routing decision agreement with spectral routing. Expected result: spectral norm achieves high correlation at minimal cost, justifying its choice.

Experiments.name: Multi-Seed Reproducibility and Variance Quantification
Experiments.description: Run all experiments across 5 random seeds (different train/val/test splits, different input orderings). Report: (1) mean accuracy, latency, and FLOPs across seeds, (2) standard deviation and 95% confidence intervals, (3) per-seed Pareto frontiers to visualize variance. Provide raw telemetry: exact hardware (GPU model, CUDA version), batch sizes, Lanczos iteration counts, wall-clock times per layer, routing decisions per sample (for first 100 test samples). Release reproducible code and trained model checkpoints.

Risk Factors and Limitations: Lanczos iteration overhead may exceed 5% target on very deep models (>100 layers) or with strict latency budgets (<10ms). Mitigation: implement early stopping (1-2 iterations) and cache spectral estimates across similar inputs.

PAC-Bayes threshold calibration requires a representative validation set (1000+ samples). On small datasets or domain shifts, calibration may fail. Mitigation: use cross-validation and report coverage explicitly; provide adaptive threshold refinement for online settings.

Spectral norm may not capture layer importance in highly nonlinear regimes (e.g., ReLU networks with sparse activations). Mitigation: validate empirically on diverse architectures; compare to gradient-based importance metrics as oracle.

Single-pass Lanczos approximation may have high variance for very small layers or layers with clustered eigenvalues. Mitigation: use adaptive iteration count (increase iterations for uncertain estimates) and quantify variance bounds.

Method assumes layer skipping does not introduce catastrophic error propagation. On models with strong layer dependencies (e.g., residual connections), skipping may fail. Mitigation: explicitly test on ResNets (with skip connections) and report per-architecture results; use residual-aware routing that accounts for skip connection structure.

Generalization across architectures and tasks unclear. Initial validation on vision (ResNet, ViT) and NLP (LLaMA); may not transfer to RNNs, GNNs, or other domains. Mitigation: frame as architecture-specific instantiation; provide modular framework for extending to new architectures.

Memory-bandwidth accounting may vary across hardware (GPU, TPU, CPU). Wall-clock latency guarantees are hardware-specific. Mitigation: provide detailed hardware specifications; benchmark on multiple GPU models (A100, H100, V100); report latency as function of memory bandwidth.

Adversarial inputs may exploit spectral routing by inducing large perturbations that change routing decisions. Mitigation: test robustness under structured perturbations (as in Experiment 5); provide certified routing bounds under bounded adversarial perturbations.
